text:
<@U08DYF4SEAE> Totally agree here, I'd love to see more discourse and imagination around this topic. I've been collecting various essays/resources on anti-colonial/post-colonial computing, if there's interest I can definitely organize and share them more formally! (and I'd love to gather more from folks here)

For example, for me one of the most radical visions of computing is Jon Corbett's work on _Indigitalization_

<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nK_Ho57GWsg>

text:
<@U0378MDUG1Y>. That sent my mind down all kinds of tangents. My people have a similar conception of time to that held by the First Nations peoples of North American. We have a whakatauki or proverb, which says, Kia whakatōmuri te haere whakamua, which translates as, "I walk backwards into the future with my eyes focused on my past'

"This whakataukī or ‘proverb’ speaks to Māori perspectives of time, where the past, the present and the future are viewed as intertwined, and life as a continuous cosmic process. Within this continuous cosmic movement, time has no restrictions – it is both past and present. The past is central to and shapes both present and future identity. From this perspective, the individual carries their past into the future. The strength of carrying one’s past into the future is that ancestors are ever present, existing both within the spiritual realm and in the physical, alongside the living as well as within the living"
<https://researchcommons.waikato.ac.nz/entities/publication/d7b6cf3c-4d4e-4f71-86f5-8fa54c905bd8>

I was excited to read a book by the Australian philosopher Huw Price, which speculated that time's arrow that leads from the past to the present, and future may not be absolute, but actually allow for the future to impact the present, especially through quantum mechanics. So he's basically vindicating the intuitions of my ancestors.
<https://global.oup.com/academic/product/times-arrow-and-archimedes-point-9780195100952?cc=nz&lang=en&#>

New developments in theoretical physics appear to confirm Huw Price's conjectures.

"“The surprising part of this project was that even after making the standard simplifying assumption to our equations describing open quantum systems, the equations still behaved the same way whether the system was moving forwards or backwards in time,” says first author Thomas Guff."
<https://tinyurl.com/unhhn4yy>

I myself got had the intuition that consciousness is underpinned by quantum mechanics and properties like superposition and entanglement, which I speculated allowed for humans in particular to project themselves into the future. I mean how is it that we can imagine a future we want, coordinate with other people, and take action to allow us to actually intersect in the same space at a specific point in time? There is increasing evidence to support this theory too.

<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SiuqsrezVf0>

attachments.fallback: Kia whakatōmuri te haere whakamua: ‘I walk backwards into the future with my eyes fixed on my past’
attachments.text: This whakataukī or ‘proverb’ speaks to Māori perspectives of time, where the past, the present and the future are viewed as intertwined, and life as a continuous cosmic process. Within this continuous cosmic movement, time has no restrictions – it is both past and present. The past is central to and shapes both present and future identity. From this perspective, the individual carries their past into the future. The strength of carrying one’s past into the future is that ancestors are ever present, existing both within the spiritual realm and in the physical, alongside the living as well as within the living. This article explores Māori perspectives of the past and the models and inspiration they offer. In this way, it provides a critique of the practices in early childhood education, highlighting the importance of cultural concepts and practices, and discusses implications for both teaching and academic practice.
attachments.title: Kia whakatōmuri te haere whakamua: ‘I walk backwards into the future with my eyes fixed on my past’

attachments.fallback: Cosmos: Theoretical physicists show that quantum systems have opposing arrows of time
attachments.text: New research shows that time can flow forwards or backwards in quantum systems, challenging our everyday perception of how time progresses.
attachments.title: Theoretical physicists show that quantum systems have opposing arrows of time
